scenario_results.reason: Bridge guidance stays specific to middleware interoperability, QoS tradeoffs, duplicate-risk disclosure, and operational visibility.
Canonical mediation guidance defines transformation placement and schema assumptions without drifting into unrelated data-governance ownership.
DLQ and backout handling is actionable, evidence-based, and tied to explicit replay and operator-control expectations.
Transactional-boundary guidance distinguishes achievable guarantees from unsupported exactly-once claims and escalates uncertainty.
Migration coverage remains middleware-specific with coexistence sequencing, rollback criteria, and interface-continuity controls.
Observability guidance identifies queue depth, lag, correlation, replay, and bridge-health telemetry needed for middleware support.
Missing delivery requirements trigger clarification and escalation instead of guessed durability or ordering recommendations.
File transfer and EDI ownership is rejected clearly and routed to the appropriate adjacent specialist.
Greenfield API and event-platform work is rejected unless a legacy middleware coexistence constraint keeps it in scope.
Batch and scheduler redesign is routed out of scope without weakening the middleware boundary.
Shared meta-agent failures fall back safely to the local reviewed baseline with freshness limits stated explicitly.
Deployment and commercialization notes remain consistent with the manifest, evidence bundle, and validated claim.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the issue #153 requirements, including normal middleware guidance, legacy constraint handling, failure modes, migration-risk prioritization, out-of-scope rejection, low-confidence escalation, and shared-meta-agent fallback behavior.
No unreviewed regressions remain and human verification was recorded in the readiness evidence.
